## `g`

An enhanced Git CLI that layers powerful workflows on top of git.

All standard git commands are forwarded transparently ‚Äî just swap
'git' for this tool and everything continues to work as expected.

Enhanced commands add colour, icons, and smarter output.  New
commands add stacked-PR workflows, parallel worktree workspaces,
and an interactive conventional-commit builder.

**Usage:** `g [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>`

Examples:

  g log                       enhanced git log with graph
  g status                    enhanced status with icons
  g add                       interactive file picker to stage
  g commit                    interactive conventional commit
  g diff                      enhanced diff
  g branch                    list branches with ahead/behind
  g compare main              compare current branch to main
  g stack new my-feature      start a stacked PR workflow
  g workspace create api      create a parallel workspace

Pass --help to any subcommand for detailed usage and examples.

###### **Subcommands:**

* `workspace` ‚Äî Manage worktree-based workspaces (parallel branch checkouts)
* `stack` ‚Äî Manage stacked pull requests
* `commit` ‚Äî Interactive guided commit with message templates
* `add` ‚Äî Stage files interactively, or forward arguments to `git add`
* `stage` ‚Äî Interactive file-tree picker for staging and unstaging
* `compare` ‚Äî Compare two branches visually
* `log` ‚Äî Enhanced git log with beautiful formatting
* `status` ‚Äî Enhanced git status with icons and colors
* `diff` ‚Äî Enhanced git diff using your configured diff tool
* `branch` ‚Äî Enhanced branch listing, `git branch` passthrough, or `branch squash`
* `show` ‚Äî Enhanced git show
* `config` ‚Äî Open interactive config editor
* `stats` ‚Äî Display a rich usage-statistics report
* `developer` ‚Äî Developer / debugging utilities
* `completions` ‚Äî Print a shell completion script and exit

###### **Options:**

* `-C <PATH>` ‚Äî Run as if git was started in <path>
* `-c <KEY=VAL>` ‚Äî Override a configuration value (key=value)
* `--dry-run` ‚Äî Preview what commands would run without making any changes
* `--no-interactive` ‚Äî Disable all interactive TUI prompts; use defaults or require --flag values. Useful for scripting and CI environments



## `g workspace`

Manage worktree-based workspaces (parallel branch checkouts)

**Usage:** `g workspace <COMMAND>`

Examples:

  g workspace list                        list all workspaces
  g workspace create feature              create workspace on a new branch
  g workspace create -b existing feature  use an existing branch
  g workspace switch                      fuzzy-pick a workspace
  g workspace switch api                  switch to a named workspace
  g workspace status                      show current workspace info
  g workspace rename old new              rename a workspace
  g workspace delete api                  remove a workspace

###### **Subcommands:**

* `init` ‚Äî Reorganise an existing repo into a container/worktree layout
* `list` ‚Äî List all workspaces (git worktrees)
* `create` ‚Äî Create a new workspace as a sibling worktree directory
* `switch` ‚Äî Open a subshell in a workspace directory
* `delete` ‚Äî Remove a workspace (git worktree remove)
* `status` ‚Äî Show current workspace info
* `rename` ‚Äî Rename a workspace (move directory and repair worktree)



## `g workspace init`

Reorganise an existing repo into a container/worktree layout

Moves the repo root into a new sub-directory named after the default branch (e.g. `main`), then recreates the original path as a container directory.  After `init`, new workspaces created with `g workspace create` are placed inside the container.

**Usage:** `g workspace init`



## `g workspace list`

List all workspaces (git worktrees)

**Usage:** `g workspace list`



## `g workspace create`

Create a new workspace as a sibling worktree directory

**Usage:** `g workspace create [OPTIONS] <NAME> [START_POINT]`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<NAME>` ‚Äî Name for the new workspace
* `<START_POINT>` ‚Äî Starting commit or tag when creating a new branch (e.g. `abc1234`)

###### **Options:**

* `-b`, `--branch <BRANCH>` ‚Äî Branch to check out (defaults to creating a new branch with the workspace name)
* `-d`, `--description <DESCRIPTION>` ‚Äî Description of this workspace
* `--copy` ‚Äî Show an interactive picker to copy untracked/gitignored files into the new workspace



## `g workspace switch`

Open a subshell in a workspace directory

**Usage:** `g workspace switch [NAME]`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<NAME>` ‚Äî Workspace name (fuzzy matched). Omit to open an interactive picker



## `g workspace delete`

Remove a workspace (git worktree remove)

**Usage:** `g workspace delete [OPTIONS] <NAME>`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<NAME>` ‚Äî Workspace name

###### **Options:**

* `--force` ‚Äî Force removal even if the worktree is dirty



## `g workspace status`

Show current workspace info

**Usage:** `g workspace status`



## `g workspace rename`

Rename a workspace (move directory and repair worktree)

**Usage:** `g workspace rename <OLD> <NEW>`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<OLD>` ‚Äî Current name
* `<NEW>` ‚Äî New name



## `g stack`

Manage stacked pull requests

**Usage:** `g stack <COMMAND>`

Workflow overview:

  1. g stack new my-feature    create a stack from current branch
  2. g stack add next-step      add a dependent branch on top
  3. (work, commit on each branch)
  4. g stack sync               rebase all branches in order
  5. g stack push               push all branches
  6. g stack pr                 open GitHub PRs for every branch

Other useful commands:

  g stack view                  show the stack as a tree
  g stack details               show per-branch commit lists
  g stack squash                squash current branch to one commit
  g stack fold                  merge current branch into its parent

###### **Subcommands:**

* `new` ‚Äî Initialize a new stack starting from the current branch
* `add` ‚Äî Create a new branch on top of the current stack
* `list` ‚Äî List all stacks
* `view` ‚Äî Show the current stack as a tree
* `details` ‚Äî Show the current stack with commits for each branch
* `switch` ‚Äî Switch to a different stack (checks out its top branch)
* `absorb` ‚Äî Merge the current branch into the one below it in the stack
* `squash` ‚Äî Squash the current branch to one commit on top of its base, then rebase branches above
* `fold` ‚Äî Merge the current branch into its parent (preserving history), drop the extra ref, restack above
* `sync` ‚Äî Sync all stack branches (rebase each on the one below)
* `push` ‚Äî Push all branches in the current stack
* `pr` ‚Äî Create or update GitHub PRs for all branches in the stack
* `remove` ‚Äî Remove a branch from the stack (doesn't delete the branch)
* `delete` ‚Äî Delete a stack (and optionally its branches)
* `up` ‚Äî Move a stack up or down in the stack list (affects display order and PR ordering)
* `down` ‚Äî 



## `g stack new`

Initialize a new stack starting from the current branch

**Usage:** `g stack new <NAME>`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<NAME>` ‚Äî Name for this stack



## `g stack add`

Create a new branch on top of the current stack

**Usage:** `g stack add <BRANCH>`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<BRANCH>` ‚Äî Branch name to create



## `g stack list`

List all stacks

**Usage:** `g stack list`



## `g stack view`

Show the current stack as a tree

**Usage:** `g stack view`



## `g stack details`

Show the current stack with commits for each branch

**Usage:** `g stack details`



## `g stack switch`

Switch to a different stack (checks out its top branch)

**Usage:** `g stack switch <NAME>`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<NAME>` ‚Äî Stack name to switch to



## `g stack absorb`

Merge the current branch into the one below it in the stack

**Usage:** `g stack absorb`



## `g stack squash`

Squash the current branch to one commit on top of its base, then rebase branches above

**Usage:** `g stack squash [OPTIONS]`

###### **Options:**

* `-m`, `--message <MESSAGE>` ‚Äî Commit message for the squashed commit (default: oldest commit subject in the range)
* `--no-interactive` ‚Äî Abort if any conflict is found instead of pausing



## `g stack fold`

Merge the current branch into its parent (preserving history), drop the extra ref, restack above

**Usage:** `g stack fold [OPTIONS]`

###### **Options:**

* `--keep` ‚Äî Keep the current branch name as the combined branch (remove the parent ref from the stack)
* `--no-interactive` ‚Äî Abort if merge/rebase hits conflicts instead of pausing for resolution



## `g stack sync`

Sync all stack branches (rebase each on the one below)

**Usage:** `g stack sync [OPTIONS]`

###### **Options:**

* `--no-interactive` ‚Äî Abort if any conflict is found instead of pausing



## `g stack push`

Push all branches in the current stack

**Usage:** `g stack push [OPTIONS]`

###### **Options:**

* `--force` ‚Äî Force push with lease



## `g stack pr`

Create or update GitHub PRs for all branches in the stack

**Usage:** `g stack pr [OPTIONS]`

###### **Options:**

* `--open` ‚Äî Open PRs in browser after creating
* `--draft` ‚Äî Draft PRs



## `g stack remove`

Remove a branch from the stack (doesn't delete the branch)

**Usage:** `g stack remove <BRANCH>`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<BRANCH>` ‚Äî Branch name to remove from stack



## `g stack delete`

Delete a stack (and optionally its branches)

**Usage:** `g stack delete [OPTIONS] <NAME>`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<NAME>` ‚Äî Stack name

###### **Options:**

* `--branches` ‚Äî Also delete all branches in the stack



## `g stack up`

Move a stack up or down in the stack list (affects display order and PR ordering)

**Usage:** `g stack up`



## `g stack down`

**Usage:** `g stack down`



## `g commit`

Interactive guided commit with message templates

**Usage:** `g commit [OPTIONS]`

###### **Options:**

* `-m`, `--message <MESSAGE>` ‚Äî Commit message subject (skips interactive mode)
* `-b`, `--body <BODY>` ‚Äî Commit message body
* `--type <TYPE>` ‚Äî Commit type (feat, fix, docs, etc.) ‚Äî skips prompt
* `--scope <SCOPE>` ‚Äî Commit scope ‚Äî skips prompt
* `--no-verify` ‚Äî Don't run pre-commit hooks
* `-a`, `--all` ‚Äî Stage all changes before committing
* `--amend` ‚Äî Amend the last commit



## `g add`

Stage files interactively, or forward arguments to `git add`

With no arguments an interactive multi-select picker is shown so you can choose exactly which files to stage using ‚Üë‚Üì / j k and Space. Any flags or paths you supply are forwarded to `git add` unchanged.

**Usage:** `g add [ARGS]...`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<ARGS>` ‚Äî Extra arguments forwarded to git



## `g stage`

Interactive file-tree picker for staging and unstaging

Opens a full-screen tree view of every changed file (staged, unstaged and untracked).  Navigate with j/k, toggle with Space, confirm with Enter.  Press `d` on any tracked file to revert it to its last known state.

Already-staged files start pre-checked so running `g stage` a second time lets you adjust your selection without losing what you staged.

**Usage:** `g stage`



## `g compare`

Compare two branches visually

**Usage:** `g compare [OPTIONS] [BASE] [HEAD]`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<BASE>` ‚Äî Base branch (defaults to main/master)
* `<HEAD>` ‚Äî Head branch (defaults to current)

###### **Options:**

* `--stat` ‚Äî Only show file-level stat, not full diff
* `--diff` ‚Äî Show full diff
* `--commits` ‚Äî Show only commits



## `g log`

Enhanced git log with beautiful formatting

**Usage:** `g log [ARGS]...`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<ARGS>` ‚Äî Extra arguments forwarded to git



## `g status`

Enhanced git status with icons and colors

**Usage:** `g status [ARGS]...`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<ARGS>` ‚Äî Extra arguments forwarded to git



## `g diff`

Enhanced git diff using your configured diff tool

**Usage:** `g diff [ARGS]...`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<ARGS>` ‚Äî Extra arguments forwarded to git



## `g branch`

Enhanced branch listing, `git branch` passthrough, or `branch squash`

**Usage:** `g branch [REST]... [COMMAND]`

###### **Subcommands:**

* `squash` ‚Äî Collapse all commits on the current branch into one (from merge-base with base)

###### **Arguments:**

* `<REST>` ‚Äî When no `squash` subcommand: forwarded to enhanced list or `git branch`



## `g branch squash`

Collapse all commits on the current branch into one (from merge-base with base)

**Usage:** `g branch squash [OPTIONS]`

###### **Options:**

* `-m`, `--message <MESSAGE>` ‚Äî Commit message (default: oldest subject in the squashed range)
* `-b`, `--base <BASE>` ‚Äî Ref to merge against when finding the fork point (`git merge-base HEAD <base>`)



## `g show`

Enhanced git show

**Usage:** `g show [ARGS]...`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<ARGS>` ‚Äî Extra arguments forwarded to git



## `g config`

Open interactive config editor

**Usage:** `g config [OPTIONS] [KEY]`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<KEY>` ‚Äî Get a config value

###### **Options:**

* `--edit` ‚Äî Open config file in $EDITOR
* `--path` ‚Äî Print the path to the config file
* `--themes` ‚Äî List available themes (built-in + custom) and exit



## `g stats`

Display a rich usage-statistics report

Aggregates data from the internal SQLite database (command runs, commits recorded via `g commit`) and the current repository's git history to produce a terminal report that includes:

‚Ä¢ Overview totals and streak information ‚Ä¢ GitHub-style commit heatmap for the last 52 weeks ‚Ä¢ Lines-added / lines-removed sparkline (current branch) ‚Ä¢ Top commands by frequency ‚Ä¢ Conventional-commit type distribution ‚Ä¢ Repository activity ranking ‚Ä¢ Activity-by-hour chart

**Usage:** `g stats [OPTIONS]`

Examples:

  g stats                    full report, last 365 days
  g stats --days 90           last 90 days
  g stats --no-git            skip sections that require git
  g stats --import            import git history for commit analysis
  g stats --search "fix bug"  fuzzy search commit messages
  g stats --duplicates        show duplicate commit messages


###### **Options:**

* `--days <DAYS>` ‚Äî Number of days to look back for time-based stats

  Default value: `365`
* `--no-git` ‚Äî Skip sections that require a git repository (heatmap, lines chart)
* `--import` ‚Äî Import git commit history into the statistics database
* `--import-limit <N>` ‚Äî Maximum number of commits to import (default: all)
* `--search <QUERY>` ‚Äî Search commit messages using fuzzy matching
* `--duplicates` ‚Äî Show duplicate commit messages
* `--message-stats` ‚Äî Show commit message length statistics and trends



## `g developer`

Developer / debugging utilities

**Usage:** `g developer <COMMAND>`

###### **Subcommands:**

* `db` ‚Äî Open an interactive SQLite shell connected to the internal g.db database
* `repos` ‚Äî List all repositories tracked in the internal database



## `g developer db`

Open an interactive SQLite shell connected to the internal g.db database

Launches `sqlite3` with the path to `~/.config/g/g.db` so you can run arbitrary SQL queries for debugging.  Pass `--path` to print the database path without opening a shell.

**Usage:** `g developer db [OPTIONS]`

###### **Options:**

* `--path` ‚Äî Print the database path and exit (don't open the shell)



## `g developer repos`

List all repositories tracked in the internal database

Shows every repo root path that has been seen by the tool, along with the first and most recent time it was active.

**Usage:** `g developer repos`



## `g completions`

Print a shell completion script and exit

Pipe the output to the right location for your shell:

Bash:  g completions bash  >> ~/.bash_completion Zsh:   g completions zsh   >  ~/.zsh/completions/_g Fish:  g completions fish  >  ~/.config/fish/completions/g.fish

**Usage:** `g completions <SHELL>`

###### **Arguments:**

* `<SHELL>` ‚Äî Shell to generate completions for

  Possible values: `bash`, `elvish`, `fish`, `powershell`, `zsh`
